fact: November 23 is exactly 32 days before Christmas, which means if you started doing one jumping jack every day until Christmas, you'd do 528 jumping jacks total!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is when squirrels go absolutely bonkers burying nuts - they can bury up to 25 nuts per hour and remember where 95% of them are hidden!
The sun sets so early around November 23 in northern places like Alaska that kids might see the sunset during their lunch break at school.
This date falls smack in the middle of Sagittarius season, which means people born now are supposedly the most adventurous and can't sit still for more than five minutes.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, November 23 is when jacaranda trees explode with purple flowers so bright they look like someone spilled grape juice all over the branches.
Arctic foxes are busy growing their thick white winter coats right now, and by this date they look like fluffy white marshmallows with legs and black button noses.
November 23 is only about 28 days away from the winter solstice in the north, which means daylight is shrinking by about 2-3 minutes every single day.
Maple trees in cold places have completely finished dropping their leaves by now, and a single big maple tree can dump over 200,000 leaves on your yard.
The moon around this time of year appears extra huge and orange when it rises because of all the particles floating in the cold, crisp air.
In places like Canada and northern United States, lakes start forming their first thin layer of ice around November 23, and it makes the coolest crackling sounds like nature's bubble wrap.
Deer are in the middle of their winter coat growth spurt right now, and each hair is actually hollow like a tiny straw to trap warm air next to their skin.
This is when evergreen trees like pine and spruce start producing extra sap to keep from freezing, making them smell super piney and Christmas-y when you walk past them.
